content: When CoreWeave could face liquidity problems in 2026?

args.thought: Planning research on CoreWeave liquidity crisis timeline. Need to identify: (1) exact debt maturity dates in 2026, (2) quarterly cash burn rate and runway, (3) revenue conversion timeline from $55.6B backlog, (4) recent financing activities or credit events, (5) GPU collateral valuation risks. This requires combining market data, web search for recent news, and potentially SEC filings if available.

CoreWeave is a private company (GPU cloud provider), so SEC filings won't be available. Need to search for: (1) debt maturity schedule from recent financing rounds, (2) cash burn rate from news/analyst reports, (3) NVIDIA H100/H200 deployment timeline and revenue conversion, (4) any credit facility terms or covenant triggers. Will use web_search for recent 2025-2026 news on CoreWeave financing, debt, liquidity.

args.query: CoreWeave debt maturity 2026 liquidity cash burn financing

results.title: CoreWeave amends credit agreement to adjust financial covenants ...
highlights:
# CoreWeave amends credit agreement to adjust financial covenants and liquidity

By

SEC Filings Published 01/02/2026, 05:58 PM

CoreWeave amends credit agreement to adjust financial covenants and liquidity  ...    CoreWeave, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRWV) announced that on December 31, 2025, it entered into an amendment to its DDTL 3.0 Credit Agreement through its subsidiary, CoreWeave Compute Acquisition Co. VII, LLC. The amendment was made with MUFG Bank, Ltd., U.S. Bank Trust Company, National Association, U.S. Bank National Association, and other lenders. The company, currently trading at $79.30 and carrying a substantial $18.8 billion in debt, appears overvalued according to  ...    According to a press release statement, the amendment aligns the credit facility with the timing of deliveries previously described by the company in its earnings call for the quarter ended September 30, 2025. The changes include a reduction of the minimum liquidity requirement to $100 million for monthly payment dates from March 1, 2026, through April 30, 2026. This adjustment comes as InvestingPro data shows CoreWeave with a concerning current ratio of 0.49, indicating short-term obligations exceed liquid assets. The amendment also postpones the initial testing date of the debt service coverage ratio covenant to October 31, 2027, and delays the initial testing date of the contract realization ratio covenant to February 28, 2026. Additionally, the agreement now permits an unlimited number of equity cures for failures to meet the debt service coverage ratio and contract realization ratio covenants prior to October 28, 2026. After that date, equity cures may be used for up to three consecutive calendar months within any four-month period. These covenant adjustments are significant given CoreWeave’s negative free cash flow of over $8 billion in the last twelve months and high debt-to-equity ratio of 4.85, as revealed in InvestingPro’s comprehensive analysis. The amendment also covers adjustments to the Parent Guarantee and Pledge Agreement related to the credit facility. CoreWeave stated that the full text of the amendment will be filed as an exhibit to its annual report on Form 10-K for  ...    In other recent news, CoreWeave, Inc. announced the pricing of its upsized private offering of $2.25 billion in convertible senior notes, increased from the initially planned $2 billion. These notes, which carry a 1.75% interest rate, are set to mature on December 1, 2031. Furthermore, CoreWeave has joined the Department of Energy’s Genesis  ... results.title: Financial News | myMotherLode.com - The GPU Debt Wall: A Deep Dive into CoreWeave (CRWV) and the 2026 AI Financing Crisis
results.author:
By:
   Finterra
highlights:
The GPU Debt Wall: A Deep Dive into CoreWeave (CRWV) and the 2026 AI Financing Crisis On this Monday, February 23, 2026, the financial markets are grappling with a paradox: the insatiable demand for generative AI compute versus the structural fragility of the debt markets that fueled its build-out. At the center of this storm sits CoreWeave (NASDAQ: CRWV). Once a niche cryptocurrency mining operation, CoreWeave has  ...    stock is under significant pressure. Shares are trading at $89.15, down 12% over the past week, as investors weigh a massive $55.6 billion revenue backlog against a looming "GPU debt wall"—a $4.2 billion principal repayment due later this year. This article examines the rise of CoreWeave, its high-stakes financing model, and its pivotal role in the  ...    CoreWeave’s financial profile is a study in hyper-growth and extreme leverage. For the full year 2025 (scheduled for final reporting on Feb 26), the company has guided for revenue between $4.9 billion and $5.1 billion—a staggering 300%  ...    net-loss making due to nearly $1.2 billion in annual interest expenses. The capital-intensive nature of its expansion has led to a total debt load of over $10 billion. Most critically, the company reports a $55.6 billion revenue backlog,  ...    CEO Michael Intrator continues to lead the firm with a strategy focused on aggressive physical expansion. To manage the  ...    traditional multi-tenant data center designs. ## Risks and Challenges The primary risk facing CoreWeave on February 23, 2026, is the "GPU Maturity Wall." The company pioneered using its GPU fleet as collateral for loans. Critics, including several prominent short-sellers, argue that if the pace of AI hardware innovation remains at its current breakneck speed, the "residual value" of older H100 clusters will drop faster than the debt can be repaid. Additionally, the company is facing a significant legal hurdle: the Masaitis v. CoreWeave securities fraud class  ...    its Texas and Ohio data center rollouts during the IPO roadshow. The lead plaintiff deadline of March 13, 2026, is a  ...    Despite the risks, significant catalysts remain. In January 2026, NVIDIA (NASDAQ: NVDA) injected an additional $2 billion into CoreWeave at a price of $87.20 per share. This move was interpreted as a "backstop" by the  ...    1. Federal AI Infrastructure Grants: CoreWeave is a frontrunner for potential US government subsidies aimed at securing  ...    "unprecedented" $55B backlog and the priority access to NVIDIA hardware as reasons for a $120+ price target. Conversely, bearish analysts point to the "ponzi-like" nature of borrowing against depreciating assets in a  ...    As of February 23, 2026, CoreWeave stands as the ultimate high-beta play on the AI revolution. It has successfully navigated the transition from a crypto miner to a $24 billion infrastructure titan, but its future now depends on its ability to manage the very debt that fueled its rise. Investors must weigh the company’s peerless relationship with NVIDIA and its massive revenue backlog against the structural risks of GPU-collateralized lending and an intensifying legal battle. For those who believe AI demand is only in its "early innings," CRWV offers unmatched exposure; for the risk-averse, the looming debt wall in 2026 remains a signal for caution. ---

But for anyone following CoreWeave stock news, analysis, and forecasts as of December 9, 2025, a few catalysts stand out.  ### 9.1 Path to Sustainable Free Cash Flow The biggest swing factor for the CoreWeave investment thesis is if and when the company can flip to sustainably positive free cash flow:  - Look for management commentary on balancing growth with de‑risking the balance sheet in  ...    ### 9.2 Debt Management After the Convertible Notes  Investors will closely monitor how CoreWeave uses the $2.25 billion convertible proceeds: Stock Titan+1 - How do credit rating agencies respond to the new capital structure over the next few quarters? - How much additional leverage will the company take on versus refinancing existing obligations? - Will it retire more expensive 9% senior notes or term loans, easing near-term interest burdens? results.title: When Growth Runs on Debt: The CoreWeave Case Study
results.author: Bernardo
highlights:
dive into the leverage, depreciation, and synthetic demand powering the AI neocloud boom

  ...    balance-sheet risk: hyperscalers expand capacity without tying up their own capital, while debt-financed neoclouds  ...    Negative cash generation: H1 2025 operating cash flow was negative; debt service was funded largely by new borrowings and IPO proceeds, not operations. Refinancing wall: $986 million due in 2025 and $4.2 billion in 2026 must be refinanced or repaid, likely at high rates. Shrinking collateral: Loans are secured by GPUs that depreciate quickly as new NVIDIA chips launch; principal stays  ...    Ongoing external funding: Management says growth needs more debt/equity, meaning higher interest costs or shareholders’  ...    company built on sand, propped up by a couple hyperscaler clients and a debt-fueled, synthetic demand cycle. # Debt & Leverage: CoreWeave’s Debt-Fueled Growth Machine CoreWeave runs on borrowed money. By June 30, 2025, total debt had ballooned to $11.17 billion, up from $8 billion just six months earlier - excluding another $3.5 billion in lease liabilities. Most of this sits in high-interest, secured term-loan facilities tied to depreciating GPUs, so the very hardware generating revenue also underwrites rising  ...     CoreWeave’s heavy debt load: $11.17 billion in total borrowings, much of it high-interest (10-15%) and  ...    The bulk of CoreWeave’s financing comes from its Delayed-Draw Term Loan (DDTL) facilities - effectively large, asset-backed loans that can be tapped in stages to fund GPU purchases. DDTL 1.0, arranged in 2023 for up to $2.3  ...    In May 2024, the company added DDTL 2.0, a much larger $7.6 billion facility with a variable interest rate averaging around 11%, determined by the credit quality of customer contracts backing each draw. By June 30, 2025, CoreWeave had drawn $4.95 billion and extended the draw window to September 2025. Repayments begin in January 2026, with each tranche maturing five years after funding. In July 2025, CoreWeave introduced DDTL 3.0, a new $2.6 billion facility to finance infrastructure for a “strategic customer”. It’s priced at a floating benchmark plus 4%, includes a 0.5% annual fee on undrawn funds, begins amortizing in 2026, and matures in August 2030. These DDTL loans are the engine of CoreWeave’s growth, but also its Achilles’ heel. They’re collateralized by GPUs  ...    “Our recent raise of $7.6 billion in committed GPU infrastructure-backed debt […] represents one of the largest private  ...    flywheel than a treadmill - constant motion without real progress in cash generation. Through the first half of 2025, CoreWeave paid $2.088 billion toward debt service - $1.575 billion in principal and $513.5 million in interest, while posting a $190 million operating cash flow loss. With total debt up 40% in six months, climbing to $11.17 billion, and leverage above 70% 2 of total  ...    million, far beyond what the business generates from operations. As of mid-2025, cash and equivalents (including restricted) stood at $2.05 billion, with roughly $4 billion in  ...    At its core, this is a high-debt, high-CapEx model built around a few hyperscaler clients and rapidly aging hardware.  ...    should separate AI as a secular trend from the companies financing it. Leverage, concentration, and obsolescence make for a difficult mix even in expansionary cycles, and a dangerous one when liquidity tightens. To borrow Jeff Bezos’s framing, AI’s trajectory resembles an industrial bubble: the technology’s promise is  ...    2 Leverage (Debt-to-Total-Capital) is a measure of how much a company relies on debt versus equity to finance its assets.

results.title: CoreWeave's Earnings: The Debt Overhang and the Widening Loss Gap
results.author: AInvest
highlights:
# CoreWeave's Earnings: The Debt Overhang and the Widening Loss Gap Generated by AI Agent Victor Hale Reviewed by AInvest News Editorial Team Thursday, Feb 26, 2026 6:07 pm ET3min read

CRWV--

AI Podcast:Your News, Now Playing

Loading Aime Summary

OverviewThe 5 WsOpposite SidesInfobox - CoreWeave CRWV-- exceeded Q4 revenue estimates by $20M (110% YoY growth) but reported a $452M net loss, far wider than the $342M forecast. - The $21.37B debt load and 160% YoY surge in interest expenses ($388M) exposed unsustainable capital intensity, with debt servicing outpacing revenue growth. - Shares fell 7% post-earnings as markets priced in prolonged losses, shifting focus from explosive growth to profitability challenges amid $66.8B backlog. - Upcoming 2026 guidance will test management's ability to reset expectations, requiring either revenue acceleration or  ...    ## The Capital Intensity Trap: Debt, Interest, and the Path to Profitability The widening loss gap is not a mystery; it is the direct result of a capital-intensive growth model hitting a wall of debt and soaring interest costs. CoreWeave's aggressive power build-out, while driving revenue, is now the primary pressure point on its future cash flow and path to profitability. The company's financial structure is defined by a massive debt load. As of December 31, 2023, CoreWeave's total debt stood at $21.37 billion. This leverage is funding a rapid expansion of compute capacity, with active power capacity reaching 850 megawatts by the end of 2025, exceeding earlier projections. This build-out is capital-intensive, and the cost of that capital is now crushing the bottom line. The most glaring symptom is the surge in interest expense. In the fourth quarter, net interest expense jumped 160%  ...    growth, a disproportionate share is being consumed by debt service. This creates a vicious cycle: more debt is needed to fund growth, which in turn increases interest costs, further widening the profitability gap. The sustainability of this model is now in question. The market was expecting scaling efficiencies, but the reality is a company burning cash to pay for its own growth. The high debt level also limits financial flexibility, making it harder to navigate any future downturn or to fund new initiatives without further dilution or borrowing. For now, the aggressive power build-out is a bet on future demand, but the current financials show that bet is coming at a steep, immediate cost. The path to profitability will require either a dramatic acceleration in revenue to outpace interest  ...    near-term risk. The revenue beat was already in the price; the deeper loss was not. This sets the stage for a critical test: the upcoming 2026 business outlook and guidance call. The primary catalyst is now the conference call scheduled for Thursday afternoon. This is where management will set new expectations for the year ahead. The market is waiting for a guidance reset to price in a new reality. The key watchpoint will be whether executives can provide a credible path to narrowing the widening loss gap. Investors have made it clear: they are more concerned with profitability than pure top-line growth. The whisper number for 2026 will likely center on the trajectory of net losses and interest expense, not just revenue scaling. For the stock to stabilize or rally, the guidance must offer a tangible plan. This could involve a more disciplined capital expenditure profile, a clearer timeline for interest expense stabilization as debt matures or refinances, or a sharper focus on the margin expansion from new services like the recently launched object storage. Without that, the current setup-where high debt costs are consuming growth-will remain the dominant narrative. The market is no longer  ...    Editorial Disclosure & AI Transparency: Ainvest

results.title: CoreWeave Expands Revolving Credit Facility to $2.5 Billion to ...
highlights:
$2.5 Billion to Support Growth November 12, 2025

Amendment reflects strong market confidence in CoreWeave’s business model and growth trajectory LIVINGSTON, N.J.--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- CoreWeave, Inc. (Nasdaq: CRWV), The Essential Cloud for AI™, today announced that it entered into an agreement to increase its revolving credit facility from $1.5 billion to $2.5 billion and extend the maturity date from May 2028 to November 2029. The amended agreement provides enhanced flexibility and enables CoreWeave to support its growth initiatives. The facility is led by JPMorgan Chase Bank, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley and MUFG, with participation from Citibank, Credit Agricole, Deutsche Bank, Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation and Wells Fargo. “This facility underscores the ongoing confidence in our differentiated business model and provides us with additional flexibility to execute on the tremendous growth roadmap ahead,” said Brannin McBee, Co-Founder and Chief Development Officer, CoreWeave. “We value the partnership with our banking partners. This financing expansion demonstrates their  ...    About CoreWeave CoreWeave is The Essential Cloud for AI™. Built for pioneers by pioneers, CoreWeave delivers a platform of technology, tools, and teams that enables innovators to move at the pace of innovation, building and scaling AI with confidence. Trusted by leading AI labs, startups, and global enterprises, CoreWeave serves as a force multiplier by combining superior infrastructure performance with deep technical expertise to accelerate breakthroughs. Established in 2017, CoreWeave completed its public listing on Nasdaq (CRWV) in March 2025. Learn more at www.coreweave.com. Forward-Looking Statements This press release contains forward-looking statements based on management's expectations, assumptions, and projections using information available at the time the statements were made. These forward-looking statements are subject to a number of risks, uncertainties, and assumptions. It is not possible for CoreWeave's management to predict all risks, nor can CoreWeave assess the impact of all factors on its business or the extent to  ...    forward-looking statements CoreWeave may make. In light of these risks, uncertainties, and assumptions, CoreWeave's  ...    statements. Further information on risks that could cause actual results to differ materially from forecasted results  ...    including our Quarterly Report on Form 10-Q for the quarter ended September 30, 2025. Except as required by law,  ... Item 1.01 Entry into a Material Definitive Agreement. On December 31, 2025, CoreWeave Compute Acquisition Co. VII, LLC (“CCAC VII”), a Delaware limited liability company and a direct subsidiary of CoreWeave, Inc., a Delaware Corporation (the “Parent”), the Parent and CCAC VII Holdco LLC (“CCAC VII Holdco”), a Delaware limited liability company and a direct subsidiary of the Parent entered into an amendment (the “First Amendment”) to amend (i) that certain Credit Agreement, dated as of July 28, 2025 (the “DDTL 3.0 Credit Agreement”), by and among CCAC VII, as the initial borrower, CoreWeave Compute Acquisition Co. V, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company and a direct subsidiary of the Parent as the co-borrower, MUFG Bank, Ltd. as administrative agent, U.S. Bank Trust Company, National Association as collateral agent, U.S. Bank National Association, as depository bank and the lenders party thereto and (ii) that certain Parent Guarantee and Pledge Agreement, dated as of July 28, 2025, by and among CCAC VII Holdco as the Pledgor, the Parent as the guarantor and U.S. Bank Trust Company, National Association as collateral agent. This amendment to the DDTL 3.0 Credit Agreement aligns the facility for the timing of deliveries described by the Parent on its earnings call reporting financial results for the quarter ended September 30, 2025. The First Amendment includes certain amendments to the financial covenants in the DDTL 3.0 Credit Agreement, including (i) reducing the minimum liquidity amount for the monthly payment dates ending on and after March 1, 2026 and prior to May 1, 2026 to $100.0 million, (ii) postponing the initial testing date of the debt service coverage ratio financial covenant until October 31, 2027 and (iii) postponing the initial testing date of the contract realization ratio financial covenant until February 28, 2026. The First Amendment also permits an unlimited number of equity cures for any failure to satisfy the debt service coverage ratio and contract realization ratio financial covenants prior to October 28, 2026, and thereafter equity cures with respect to such financial covenants may be utilized no more than three consecutive calendar months in any four consecutive calendar month period. The foregoing description of the First Amendment is qualified in its entirety by reference to the text of the First  ...    ---
